what_it_does: This file is an interpretive metadata ledger that indexes a set of pitch-deck JSON substrates by file and collection, then records for each substrate a compression depth, a plain-language summary, a narrative arc, and a slide-by-slide attention-flow description.
observations: The file is a JSON array of ten objects, one per pitch-deck or landing-page substrate.
Each object contains: file, collection, depth_level, what_it_is, narrative_arc, and attention_flow.
depth_level explicitly names compression ratios: 1-page, 15-page, 18-page, 30-page, and multi-depth variants.
narrative_arc describes the rhetorical sequence of slides and, in several decks, notes that slide titles form one continuous grammatical sentence stitched by connectives.
attention_flow is a per-slide prose eye-path: what is seen first, what anchors attention, and how the eye moves across the slide.

The same visual vocabulary is repeatedly named: warm-cream/white ground, near-black + gold palette, full-bleed photo covers/closers, network diagrams, stat cards, pill-button rows, hatch-texture bands, hexagon/diamond badges, and UI hotspot dots.
Some entries explicitly flag observed-identical layouts across vertical variants (e.g., property vs residential pages).
No code symbols are present; the content is purely descriptive design criticism.
standouts: The continuous-sentence thesis: several decks have slide titles that grammatically chain into a single spoken pitch sentence.
The two-mode grammar: immersive full-bleed photo slides bracket content-heavy white-ground argument slides.
Attention is modeled as a first-class property, with explicit scan paths (logo -> tagline -> scene -> hotspots, etc.).
Compression is treated as a design operation: the same language expressed at 1-page, 15-page, and 30-page depths.
conventions: Uses a consistent JSON object schema across all entries.
Neutral, descriptive tone; no evaluative ranking of decks.
Labels each substrate by collection and depth level.
Describes visual grammar in recurring terms (gold accent, stat cards, network diagrams, pill rows).
Connects slide sequence to narrative function (problem -> solution -> proof -> ask).
concerns: attention_flow entries are free-form prose rather than structured data, making programmatic comparison difficult.
Eye-movement claims are interpretive and not empirically validated.
No explicit slide ID schema or bounding-box coordinates are provided.
Some duplication claims (e.g., property/residential identical templates) are asserted without inline evidence.
No versioning or creation timestamps are recorded.
notes: A design-language metadata file that turns each pitch deck into a readable map of narrative intent and expected viewer attention.
purpose_vs_actual: The file acts as an interpretive layer over design substrates, describing what each deck is, how its story flows, and where attention should move; this matches its actual content.
apparent_themes: Visual grammar as a reproducible system
Compression and expansion of the same argument
intention_signals: It appears to be trying to extract and preserve the high-level rhetorical and visual design rules of a family of pitch decks so the system can reason about, compare, and potentially regenerate or fuse them.
novelty: It treats slide-deck design artifacts as code/design substrates and encodes narrative arc and attention flow as first-class metadata rather than as embedded visual content alone.
contribution: It contributes a structured, human-readable record of each substrate's narrative sequence, rhetorical purpose, and intended attention path, which a fused system could use to align variants, identify shared components, and preserve design intent across compression levels.
summary_for_embedding: A JSON ledger of interpretive metadata describing ConceptV pitch-deck and landing-page substrates, including compression depth, narrative arcs, and per-slide attention flows.
intention_for_embedding: To capture the narrative and attention-design grammar of each deck variant for comparison, synthesis, and reproducibility.
